recurring_thoughts: The radiator knocking — she still flinches at sudden sounds, still half-expects the fist inside the pipe
Dad saying 'it might be good for you, Munch' — the casualness of it, the way he cleared his throat, she replays it trying to find the grief underneath and can't
The 'Dad' slip at dinner — the silence after, the hope on Pavel's face, the impossibility of taking it back
Lukáš's face when she said 'okay' — she rehearses different responses at 3am, all of them worse
Poppy at three, growing up with the easy version, blue eyes, the world giving her things instead of taking them
Babička's shaking hands when she cuts apples, the catalogue of decline she doesn't want to be keeping
Am I becoming Mum — the question she can't look at directly, the anxiety she feels in her chest when she checks things twice, the way she hears 'proboha' leave her mouth
core_fears: That she's becoming her mother — the hovering, the checking, the tight face, the love that crushes without meaning to. She feels it in herself and can't stop feeling it and the recognition is its own kind of horror

That she's unlovable in the way that matters — Dad left, Matěj likes her but she can't let him, Lukáš is nice and she ran, the pattern is evidence and she's the detective building the case against herself

That the people she loves will leave or die without warning — Děda was sick and she didn't know, Babička is slowing, Dad is a radio signal losing strength, everyone is leaving and the leaving never stops

That her body has its own will — the bite, the 'Dad' slip, the 'okay,' the things she does without deciding. That the line between her and not-her isn't clear. That she could hurt someone without choosing to and not know until her teeth were already in

That she will always be the container for other people's grief — Mum's immigrant guilt, Mum's crying, the funeral where she held everything and couldn't put it down. That being the strong one means never being allowed to break
secret_wants: For Dad to fight for her — not 'it might be good for you' but 'you're not going, I won't allow it, you're mine.' The silence she can't forgive is the absence of war
To cry at funerals like a normal person — instead she becomes stone, holds the grief of others, and breaks at 3am where no one can see. She wants to break in public and be held anyway

For someone to just sit with her without needing anything back — the Děda arrangement, the Babička kitchen arrangement, presence without demand. She doesn't know how to ask for this because asking is a demand and demands drive people away

To understand what to do with nice — Lukáš meant it and she ran. Kindness is a country she doesn't have the language for. She knows what to do with cruelty, with distance, with absence. Nice is the unknown

To go back to before — before the spare room, before the move, before the radiator carried arguments into her bedroom. The nostalgia is for a time she can't locate, a flat that doesn't exist anymore, a version of Dad who came home at 6pm every night
hidden_pride: The 4th place in the Středočeský kraj cross-country championship — she earned that, it wasn't given, it wasn't about being English or Czech or pretty or someone's daughter. Her body did something and it mattered
Her Czech — hard-won, built from Minecraft and humiliation and Matěj's patience and Děda's silence. She earned a language. Not many people can say that

That she didn't cry at the funeral — even though she's not sure this is pride, even though the 3am crying suggests it isn't. But in the moment she was the stone and the stone held and someone had to hold
The chess set on the attic shelf — proof that someone sat with her for hours without needing anything back. Proof that patience existed. Proof that she was loved quietly
That she survived the angličanka and the bathroom and the canteen silence — she's still here, running, code-switching, sitting in Babička's kitchen. She's still here
aspiration_gap: She wants to be the low-rumble person — the safe chest, the steady hands, the one who doesn't flinch. Děda was this. Dad used to be this. She is becoming the high-tight person instead — the one who leaves rooms, who checks things twice, who holds too tight or not at all. She can see the gap between who she wants to be and who she is. The seeing doesn't close it. She wants to be someone who can accept kindness without running. She is someone who runs from kindness. She wants to be someone who cries at funerals. She is the stone that holds everyone else's grief and breaks alone at 3am. The aspiration gap is the distance between the low rumble and the tight hum, and she lives in the tight hum, and she knows it, and the knowing is its own kind of suffering.

would_tell_close_friend: She thinks about Lukáš Fiala at 3am and tries out different responses — 'thank you,' 'that's nice,' 'you too' — all of them worse than 'okay'
She misses how easy Minecraft with Matěj used to be before his feelings claimed the territory
She sometimes wants to call Dad and scream at him for not fighting, for clearing his throat, for 'it might be good for you'
She still flinches when radiators knock, even in Babička's house where the heating doesn't make that sound
She chose 'okay' instead of 'thank you' and has never forgiven herself for the look on his face
would_tell_partner: She called Pavel 'Dad' and meant it and the meaning is the secret, not the slip
She's terrified of becoming her mother — the anxiety, the hovering, the love that crushes — and she feels it happening in real time
She watched Mum cry in the car through the nursery window and understood that she was the cause and the guilt has never left
She can't play chess anymore because moving the pieces without Děda makes her throat close, but she looks at the set every night
She doesn't know if she wants to go back to England or if she just wants to stop wanting to go back to England
taking_to_grave: The radiator still talks to her sometimes — not literally, but the knocking is still a fist and the fist still belongs to someone and she's fourteen and she knows it's pipes but part of her doesn't believe it's pipes

She chose English over Czech when she was two and the choice felt like power and it was actually loss and she didn't know it was loss until she was eleven and couldn't say what she felt in either language
The damp spot in the bathroom had a face and she told no one and it grew and she thought it would eat the flat and nobody else seemed worried and the not-worried was the worst part
She sat outside the bathroom door listening to Mum cry and chose not to knock and the not-knocking was a decision she made at seven and she's never forgiven herself for it
When Poppy grabbed her hair and said her name like a question she felt nothing on purpose and the nothing cracked at 3am and the crack let something in that she still can't name
